part_subtitle: Rational Chemical Control of Tuta absoluta

items.text: Chapter Ten
Integrated Management of Tuta absoluta
Introduction
Insecticides remain one of the tools used to manage Tuta absoluta, but they are not the sole foundation of the program and do not provide sustainable management when used alone.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Insecticides remain one of the tools used to manage Tuta absoluta, but they are not the sole foundation of the program and do not provide sustainable management when used alone.

items.text: The pest possesses several characteristics that make continuous reliance on insecticides vulnerable to failure, most importantly:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The pest possesses several characteristics that make continuous reliance on insecticides vulnerable to failure, most importantly:

items: A short life cycle under suitable temperatures.
Larvae concealed inside mines in leaves, stems, and fruits.
Continued moth immigration from neighboring fields.
Exposure of several successive generations to insecticides within the same season.
Documented resistance to several mode-of-action groups.
Difficulty reaching advanced larvae inside fruits.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A short life cycle under suitable temperatures.
[[prepared]]Larvae concealed inside [[term:mines]]mines[[/term]] in leaves, stems, and fruits.
[[prepared]]Exposure of several successive generations to insecticides within the same season.

items.text: Chemical control should therefore be used as a targeted intervention within an integrated pest-management program, after preventive, monitoring, cultural, biological, and behavioural measures have been implemented.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Chemical control should therefore be used as a targeted intervention within an integrated pest-management program, after preventive, monitoring, cultural, biological, and behavioural measures have been implemented.

items.text: Success of chemical control is not measured by the number of sprays or the rapid knockdown of some larvae, but by its ability to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Success of chemical control is not measured by the number of sprays or the rapid knockdown of some larvae, but by its ability to:

items: Prevent infestation of growing points and clusters.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Prevent infestation of growing points and clusters.

items.text: When Is Chemical Intervention Justified?
Chemical intervention becomes justified when monitoring data indicate that the pest population is approaching a level that available preventive and biological measures cannot contain in time.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Chemical intervention becomes justified when monitoring data indicate that the pest population is approaching a level that available preventive and biological measures cannot contain in time.

items.text: The decision depends on a group of indicators, including:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The decision depends on a group of indicators, including:

items: Presence of newly laid eggs or eggs close to hatching.
Presence of live larvae in more than one sector.
Appearance of infestation in growing points or buds.
Presence of advanced larvae near clusters.
An immigration wave from an old field.
The pest approaching the local intervention threshold.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Presence of newly laid eggs or eggs close to hatching.
[[prepared]]Presence of live larvae in more than one sector.
[[prepared]]Appearance of infestation in growing points or buds.
[[prepared]]An immigration wave from an old field.
[[prepared]]The pest approaching the local intervention threshold.

items.text: Chemical intervention is not justified solely by:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Chemical intervention is not justified solely by:

items: Fear of possible infestation without evidence.
A commercial recommendation unsupported by field inspection.
Repeating the previous season’s program without evaluating current conditions.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A commercial recommendation unsupported by field inspection.
[[prepared]]Repeating the previous season’s program without evaluating current conditions.

items.text: Scientific Questions Before Selecting an Insecticide
Before choosing any active ingredient, the following questions should be answered:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Before choosing any [[term:active-ingredient]]active ingredient[[/term]], the following questions should be answered:

items: 1. What is the dominant stage: eggs, young larvae, or advanced larvae?
2. Are larvae still in leaves or have they entered growing points and fruits?
4. Which mode-of-action group was previously used?
5. Is there evidence of reduced susceptibility to that group?
6. Which natural enemies are present in the field?
7. How much time remains until harvest?
8. What is the registered preharvest interval?
9. What is the maximum number of seasonal applications?
10. Is the active ingredient currently registered on tomato and against the pest in the country?
11. Is it compatible with supply-market, processing-factory, or export requirements?
12. Is there a less harmful method capable of achieving the same result?
prepared_items: [[prepared]]1. What is the dominant stage: eggs, young larvae, or advanced larvae?
[[prepared]]2. Are larvae still in leaves or have they entered growing points and fruits?
[[prepared]]4. Which mode-of-action group was previously used?
[[prepared]]5. Is there evidence of reduced susceptibility to that group?
[[prepared]]6. Which natural enemies are present in the field?
[[prepared]]7. How much time remains until harvest?
[[prepared]]8. What is the registered preharvest interval?
[[prepared]]9. What is the maximum number of seasonal applications?
[[prepared]]10. Is the active ingredient currently registered on tomato and against the pest in the country?
[[prepared]]11. Is it compatible with supply-market, processing-factory, or export requirements?
[[prepared]]12. Is there a less harmful method capable of achieving the same result?

items.text: An insecticide choice is not correct if it is effective against the pest but unregistered, incompatible with harvest timing, or highly disruptive to the biological-control program.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An insecticide choice is not correct if it is effective against the pest but unregistered, incompatible with harvest timing, or highly disruptive to the biological-control program.

items.text: Optimum Target Stage
Newly hatched larvae and the first and second instars are the best stages for chemical control in most programs.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Newly hatched larvae and the first and second instars are the best stages for chemical control in most programs.

items.text: These larvae are:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These larvae are:

items: More sensitive to many insect-growth regulators and selective insecticides.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]More sensitive to many insect-growth regulators and selective insecticides.

items.text: Third- and fourth-instar larvae are more difficult to control because of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Third- and fourth-instar larvae are more difficult to control because of:

items: Entry into growing points, stems, and fruits.
Greater damage before the active ingredient takes effect.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Entry into growing points, stems, and fruits.
[[prepared]]Greater damage before the active ingredient takes effect.

items.text: The program should therefore aim to prevent the larval population from reaching advanced instars, rather than waiting for large larvae to appear and then attempting to kill them.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The program should therefore aim to prevent the larval population from reaching advanced instars, rather than waiting for large larvae to appear and then attempting to kill them.

items.text: Spray Timing in Relation to Eggs and Hatching
The optimum timing depends on the mode of action.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The optimum timing depends on the [[term:mode-of-action]]mode of action[[/term]].

items.text: Active Ingredients Acting Through Feeding
These are applied before hatching or at its onset so that residues are present on tissues when larvae begin feeding.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These are applied before hatching or at its onset so that residues are present on tissues when larvae begin feeding.

items.text: Active Ingredients Affecting Growth and Molting
These are more effective when young instars are exposed before approaching molt or entering deep tissues.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These are more effective when young instars are exposed before approaching molt or entering deep tissues.

items.text: Active Ingredients with Contact or Translaminar Activity
These require coverage of egg sites and recent mines, and good coverage does not compensate for very late application.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These require coverage of egg sites and recent mines, and good coverage does not compensate for very late application.

items.text: Hatching time is determined using:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Hatching time is determined using:

items.text: Why Is Targeting Adult Moths Alone Insufficient?
Some insecticides can kill adult moths, but reliance on this effect alone is insufficient because:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some insecticides can kill adult moths, but reliance on this effect alone is insufficient because:

items: Some females may already have mated and laid eggs.
New moths may enter after spraying.
Eggs, larvae, and pupae are present simultaneously.
Moths may remain concealed within the canopy.
Their activity period may differ from application timing.
Broad-spectrum adulticides may harm natural enemies.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Some females may already have mated and laid eggs.
[[prepared]]Moths may remain concealed within the canopy.
[[prepared]]Their activity period may differ from application timing.

items.text: The decision should therefore focus mainly on preventing hatching and reducing young larvae, while adult control remains a supplementary element in specific situations.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The decision should therefore focus mainly on preventing hatching and reducing young larvae, while adult control remains a supplementary element in specific situations.

items.text: Importance of Application Technique
An insecticide with proven efficacy may fail because of poor application rather than weakness of the active ingredient.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An insecticide with proven efficacy may fail because of poor application rather than weakness of the active ingredient.

items.text: Important factors include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Important factors include:

items: Distribution of spray between field edges and the plant interior.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Distribution of spray between field edges and the plant interior.

items.text: Tuta absoluta management requires coverage of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tuta absoluta management requires coverage of:

items.text: Increasing spray pressure does not automatically improve control and may cause:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Increasing spray pressure does not automatically improve control and may cause:

items: Formation of very fine droplets lost outside the field.
Increased exposure of workers and neighboring areas.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Formation of very fine droplets lost outside the field.
[[prepared]]Increased exposure of workers and neighboring areas.

items.text: Sprayer Calibration
The sprayer should be calibrated before insecticide use to determine:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The sprayer should be calibrated before insecticide use to determine:

items.text: Calibration includes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Calibration includes:

items: 9. Reviewing deposition on lower leaf surfaces and growing points.
10. Recalibrating when plant size or nozzle type changes.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]9. Reviewing deposition on lower leaf surfaces and growing points.
[[prepared]]10. Recalibrating when plant size or nozzle type changes.

items.text: A sprayer calibrated at the beginning of the season should not be assumed to remain suitable after canopy volume has doubled.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A sprayer calibrated at the beginning of the season should not be assumed to remain suitable after canopy volume has doubled.

items.text: Spray-Water Volume
Spray-water volume should be determined according to plant size, production system, and equipment rather than a fixed number throughout the season.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Spray-water volume should be determined according to plant size, production system, and equipment rather than a fixed number throughout the season.

items.text: It should achieve:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It should achieve:

items: Minimal loss of most spray to the soil.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Minimal loss of most spray to the soil.

items.text: Excessively low volume leaves untreated areas, whereas excessive volume causes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Excessively low volume leaves untreated areas, whereas excessive volume causes:

items: Potentially elevated residues in some sites.
Loss of active ingredient before fixation on leaves.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Loss of active ingredient before fixation on leaves.

items.text: Spray-Water Quality
Some active ingredients or formulations may be affected by water characteristics, including:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some active ingredients or formulations may be affected by water characteristics, including:

items.text: The product label should be reviewed before adjusting water.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The product label should be reviewed before adjusting water.

items.text: Acids or acidifying agents should not be added randomly because:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Acids or acidifying agents should not be added randomly because:

items: Some active ingredients are more stable within a specific pH range.
Excessive adjustment may cause degradation or phytotoxicity.
Some mixtures react with fertilizers or copper.
Tank pH alone does not represent overall solution quality.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Some active ingredients are more stable within a specific pH range.
[[prepared]]Excessive adjustment may cause degradation or phytotoxicity.
[[prepared]]Some mixtures react with fertilizers or copper.
[[prepared]]Tank pH alone does not represent overall solution quality.

items.text: When several components are used, a small compatibility test should be conducted before preparing the full tank.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When several components are used, a small compatibility test should be conducted before preparing the full tank.

items.text: Adjuvants
Adjuvants include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Adjuvants include:

items.text: They may improve coverage or persistence for some products, but may also cause:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They may improve coverage or persistence for some products, but may also cause:

items: Damage to spores in microbial products.

items.text: An adjuvant should therefore be used only when recommended on the product label or when compatibility has been demonstrated in a documented test.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An adjuvant should therefore be used only when recommended on the product label or when compatibility has been demonstrated in a documented test.

items.text: Concept of Mode of Action
Mode of action is the principal physiological site or pathway affected by an active ingredient within the insect.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mode of action is the principal physiological site or pathway affected by an active ingredient within the insect.

items.text: The Insecticide Resistance Action Committee, IRAC, classifies insecticides into numbered groups.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The [[term:resistance]]Insecticide Resistance[[/term]] Action Committee, [[term:irac]]IRAC[[/term]], classifies insecticides into numbered groups.

items.text: Resistance-management programs are built around the mode-of-action group number, not:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Resistance-management programs are built around the mode-of-action group number, not:

items.text: Two products may have different trade names but belong to the same group, and switching between them does not constitute true rotation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Two products may have different trade names but belong to the same group, and switching between them does not constitute true rotation.

items.text: Mode-of-Action Groups Used or Studied Against Lepidoptera
The following table presents groups of scientific importance. Mention of an active ingredient does not imply that it is currently registered against Tuta absoluta or on tomato in Egypt.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following table presents groups of scientific importance. Mention of an active ingredient does not imply that it is currently registered against Tuta absoluta or on tomato in Egypt.

headers: Target Site or Mode of Action
rows: Relatively broad spectrum; resistance common in pests
Broad effects on natural enemies; registrations vary greatly
High resistance risk and strong effects on beneficial organisms
Used mainly against sucking pests in tomato; not automatically primary Tuta options
Same group despite different active ingredients
Same group; not independent rotation partners
Microbial disruptors of insect midgut membranes
Must be ingested and targets young instars
Respiratory activity; may strongly affect non-target organisms
More suitable for young instars and slower acting
Different subgroup but principal group number remains 22
All belong to group 28 and do not represent true rotation among themselves
Newer active ingredients; use only when registered and locally effective

This group includes:

This group includes:

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They act by modulating glutamate-gated chloride channels, causing disruption of nerve transmission and paralysis.

Bt is classified by IRAC as a microbial disruptor of insect midgut membranes.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Bt is classified by IRAC as a microbial disruptor of insect midgut membranes.

Potential compatibility with natural enemies when demonstrated.

items.text: Chlorfenapyr — IRAC 13
Chlorfenapyr acts by uncoupling oxidative phosphorylation and disrupting energy production.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Chlorfenapyr acts by uncoupling oxidative phosphorylation and disrupting energy production.

items.text: Its mode of action differs from that of common neurotoxic insecticides, but it is not an unrestricted solution. The following should be considered:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Its mode of action differs from that of common neurotoxic insecticides, but it is not an unrestricted solution. The following should be considered:

items.text: It should not be repeatedly used as a rescue solution following failure of other groups because repetition increases selection pressure against it.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It should not be repeatedly used as a rescue solution following failure of other groups because repetition increases selection pressure against it.

items.text: Inhibitors of Chitin Biosynthesis — IRAC 15
Benzoylurea insecticides include active ingredients such as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Benzoylurea insecticides include active ingredients such as:

items.text: They interfere with synthesis of chitin required for molting.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They interfere with synthesis of chitin required for molting.

items.text: They are more suitable:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They are more suitable:

items.text: They may not provide rapid mortality because larvae may be affected at the following molt.

items.text: Different active ingredients within group 15 do not represent rotation in mode of action.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Different active ingredients within group 15 do not represent rotation in mode of action.

This group includes active ingredients such as:

items.text: They mimic the molting-hormone signal and induce an abnormal molting process that the larva cannot complete.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]They mimic the molting-hormone signal and induce an abnormal molting process that the larva cannot complete.

items.text: They act more specifically against larvae than adults and should be applied:

items.text: Their efficacy should not be judged after only a few hours.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Their efficacy should not be judged after only a few hours.

Indoxacarb is an oxadiazine and acts as a voltage-dependent sodium-channel blocker.

items.text: Metaflumizone in subgroup 22B should not be regarded as a complete change in the principal mode-of-action group number when designing a conservative resistance-management program. Guidance concerning subgroups should be reviewed.

Most conventional neonicotinoids belong to group 4A.

Some oils, soaps, and botanical extracts may affect exposed stages through:

A commercial product may contain:

items.text: Mode-of-Action Window Approach
Mode-of-Action Window Approach
The window system divides the season into periods corresponding approximately to one pest generation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The window system divides the season into periods corresponding approximately to one pest generation.

items.text: Within each window:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Within each window:

items: An active ingredient or active ingredients from one mode-of-action group may be used according to the label.
The following window then uses a different effective group.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]An active ingredient or active ingredients from one mode-of-action group may be used according to the label.
[[prepared]]The following window then uses a different effective group.

items.text: The fundamental rule is:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The fundamental rule is:

items.text: Do not expose two successive pest generations to the same group.
The traditional IRAC model for Tuta absoluta has used windows of approximately 30 days, based on the short generation time under favorable conditions.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The traditional IRAC model for Tuta absoluta has used windows of approximately 30 days, based on the short generation time under favorable conditions.

items.text: However, this period is not fixed in all regions because generation time changes with:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, this period is not fixed in all regions because generation time changes with:

items.text: Windows should therefore be adjusted using monitoring and local thermal data.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Windows should therefore be adjusted using monitoring and local thermal data.

items.text: Avoiding Repetition of the Group Across Generations
If group 28 is used in one window, it should not be repeated in the following window even if the active ingredient changes from:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]If group 28 is used in one window, it should not be repeated in the following window even if the active ingredient changes from:

items.text: Similarly:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Similarly:

items: Spinosad and Spinetoram are both group 5.
Abamectin and Emamectin benzoate are both group 6.
Lufenuron and Chlorfluazuron are both group 15.

items.text: Rotation should therefore be constructed using group numbers such as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Rotation should therefore be constructed using group numbers such as:

items.text: Rather than trade names.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Rather than trade names.

items.text: This is only an illustrative sequence of different groups and is not a prescriptive program or evidence that the active ingredients are registered or suitable for a particular field.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is only an illustrative sequence of different groups and is not a prescriptive program or evidence that the active ingredients are registered or suitable for a particular field.

items.text: Number of Applications Within a Window
Some product labels may permit more than one application within the same window.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some product labels may permit more than one application within the same window.

items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The number of applications is determined by:

items: The maximum stated on the label.

items.text: The maximum permitted number should not be used automatically. It is a limit that must not be exceeded, not a compulsory target.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The maximum permitted number should not be used automatically. It is a limit that must not be exceeded, not a compulsory target.

items.text: Premixes and Tank Mixtures
Mixtures are not an automatic solution to resistance.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mixtures are not an automatic solution to resistance.

items.text: For a mixture to provide a potential resistance-management benefit, it should:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]For a mixture to provide a potential resistance-management benefit, it should:

items: Include two active ingredients each independently effective against the target stage.
Expose the target stage to both active ingredients simultaneously.
Have relatively similar persistence of both components.
Not contain an active ingredient to which high resistance already exists.

items: Both active ingredients belong to the same IRAC group.
One active ingredient is ineffective against Tuta.
One active ingredient is used below an effective dose.
The same mixture is repeated throughout the season.
The mixture is used to compensate for poor timing or coverage.

Random mixing of three or four active ingredients can cause:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Random mixing of three or four active ingredients can cause:

items: Difficulty identifying the cause of success or failure.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Difficulty identifying the cause of success or failure.

items.text: Definition of Insecticide Resistance

Insecticide resistance is a heritable change in the susceptibility of a pest population that results in repeated failure of a product to achieve the expected level of control when used according to the label.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Insecticide resistance is a heritable change in the susceptibility of a pest population that results in repeated failure of a product to achieve the expected level of control when used according to the label.

items.text: Resistance is not defined by:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Resistance is not defined by:

items: Immigration of new moths after spraying.

items.text: Application-related causes of failure should be excluded before resistance is declared.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Application-related causes of failure should be excluded before resistance is declared.

items.text: Resistance Mechanisms
Tuta absoluta may develop several resistance mechanisms.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tuta absoluta may develop several resistance mechanisms.

items.text: Target-Site Alteration
A heritable change occurs in the protein or receptor targeted by the insecticide, reducing binding of the active ingredient.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A heritable change occurs in the protein or receptor targeted by the insecticide, reducing binding of the active ingredient.

items.text: Metabolic Resistance
The insect’s capacity to detoxify the active ingredient increases through enzymes such as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The insect’s capacity to detoxify the active ingredient increases through enzymes such as:

items.text: Reduced Penetration
Cuticular properties change, reducing movement of the active ingredient into the body.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Cuticular properties change, reducing movement of the active ingredient into the body.

items.text: Behavioural Change
The insect may reduce contact with treated areas or alter its response.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The insect may reduce contact with treated areas or alter its response.

items.text: A population may carry more than one mechanism, resulting in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A population may carry more than one mechanism, resulting in:

items: Different responses among active ingredients within one group or among different groups.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Different responses among active ingredients within one group or among different groups.

items.text: Cross-Resistance
Cross-resistance occurs when one mechanism confers resistance to more than one active ingredient.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Cross-resistance occurs when one mechanism confers resistance to more than one active ingredient.

items.text: It is more likely among active ingredients that:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It is more likely among active ingredients that:

items: Are detoxified by the same metabolic enzymes.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Are detoxified by the same metabolic enzymes.

items.text: Metabolic cross-resistance may also occur between different groups despite different IRAC numbers.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Metabolic cross-resistance may also occur between different groups despite different IRAC numbers.

items.text: Numerical rotation among groups is therefore insufficient when local tests demonstrate cross-resistance.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Numerical rotation among groups is therefore insufficient when local tests demonstrate cross-resistance.

items.text: Distinguishing Resistance from Application Failure
When a treatment fails, the following should be reviewed:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When a treatment fails, the following should be reviewed:

items: 2. Is the active ingredient registered against the stage present?
3. Were larvae young or already inside fruits?
6. Did the spray reach lower leaf surfaces and growing points?
7. Did rainfall or overhead irrigation occur?
8. Were the water or tank mixture incompatible?
10. Had the group already been repeated?
11. Is moth immigration occurring from a neighboring field?
12. Are the mines new, or is visible damage old?
13. Were larvae evaluated after sufficient time for the active ingredient to act?

items.text: Increasing the dose or shortening the interval automatically when resistance is suspected is not recommended because it may increase selection and risk without restoring efficacy.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Increasing the dose or shortening the interval automatically when resistance is suspected is not recommended because it may increase selection and risk without restoring efficacy.

items.text: Susceptibility Testing
IRAC developed a standardized method for testing susceptibility of Tuta absoluta larvae known as Method No. 022.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]IRAC developed a standardized method for testing susceptibility of Tuta absoluta larvae known as Method No. 022.

items.text: It is based on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It is based on:

items: Rearing them to the F1 generation where possible.
Using uniform second-instar larvae approximately 4–5 mm long.
Dipping tomato leaves in specified insecticide concentrations.
Recording mortality after the appropriate assessment period.
Comparing results with a susceptible baseline or other populations.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Rearing them to the F1 generation where possible.
[[prepared]]Using uniform second-instar larvae approximately 4–5 mm long.
[[prepared]]Dipping tomato leaves in specified insecticide concentrations.
[[prepared]]Recording mortality after the appropriate assessment period.
[[prepared]]Comparing results with a susceptible baseline or other populations.

items.text: The test determines whether reduced efficacy is associated with a genuine change in susceptibility rather than poor field application.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The test determines whether reduced efficacy is associated with a genuine change in susceptibility rather than poor field application.

items.text: Management of Suspected Resistance
When resistance is suspected:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When resistance is suspected:

items: 5. Use a different group demonstrated to be locally effective.
8. Notify the technical manager or competent authority.
9. Conduct a susceptibility test where possible.
10. Avoid issuing a definitive resistance conclusion before verification.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]5. Use a different group demonstrated to be locally effective.
[[prepared]]8. Notify the technical manager or competent authority.
[[prepared]]9. Conduct a susceptibility test where possible.
[[prepared]]10. Avoid issuing a definitive resistance conclusion before verification.

items.text: Doses above the registered rate should not be used in an attempt to overcome resistance.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Doses above the registered rate should not be used in an attempt to overcome resistance.

items.text: Protection of Natural Enemies
Chemical treatment may kill:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Chemical treatment may kill:

items.text: Impacts can be reduced through:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Impacts can be reduced through:

items: Selecting a more selective active ingredient.
Applying treatment only when the threshold is exceeded.
Treating infestation foci instead of the entire field where possible.
Respecting the required interval before reintroducing a natural enemy.
Using the minimum necessary number of applications.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Applying treatment only when the threshold is exceeded.
[[prepared]]Treating infestation foci instead of the entire field where possible.
[[prepared]]Respecting the required interval before reintroducing a natural enemy.
[[prepared]]Using the minimum necessary number of applications.

items.text: An active ingredient should not be described as generally safe to natural enemies because susceptibility differs among parasitoids and predators and between adults and immature stages.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An active ingredient should not be described as generally safe to natural enemies because susceptibility differs among parasitoids and predators and between adults and immature stages.

items.text: Preharvest Interval
Pre-Harvest Interval — PHI
The preharvest interval is the minimum time required between the last insecticide application and crop harvest.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The preharvest interval is the minimum time required between the last insecticide application and crop harvest.

items.text: It is determined according to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It is determined according to:

items.text: The PHI should not be transferred from:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The PHI should not be transferred from:

items: One product to another containing the same active ingredient in a different formulation.
A research study to a commercial program.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]One product to another containing the same active ingredient in a different formulation.
[[prepared]]A research study to a commercial program.

items.text: The PHI stated on the label of the registered product should be followed, while recognizing that the supply market may impose requirements more stringent than local regulations.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The PHI stated on the label of the registered product should be followed, while recognizing that the supply market may impose requirements more stringent than local regulations.

items.text: Maximum Residue Limit
Maximum Residue Limit — MRL
The maximum residue limit is the highest legally permitted concentration of a pesticide residue in food, usually expressed in milligrams per kilogram.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The maximum residue limit is the highest legally permitted concentration of a pesticide residue in food, usually expressed in milligrams per kilogram.

items.text: An MRL is not a safe dose for adding pesticide to food. It is a regulatory limit derived from:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An MRL is not a safe dose for adding pesticide to food. It is a regulatory limit derived from:

items.text: Limits may differ among:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Limits may differ among:

items.text: The target market should therefore be identified before the spray program is developed.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The target market should therefore be identified before the spray program is developed.

items.text: Relationship Between PHI and MRL
Compliance with the registered PHI reduces the probability of exceeding the MRL, but does not guarantee compliance when misuse occurs.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Compliance with the registered PHI reduces the probability of exceeding the MRL, but does not guarantee compliance when misuse occurs.

items.text: Residues may increase because of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Residues may increase because of:

items: Poor coordination between spray and harvest teams.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Poor coordination between spray and harvest teams.

items.text: Residue management therefore depends on complete compliance with good agricultural practice, not the PHI alone.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Residue management therefore depends on complete compliance with good agricultural practice, not the PHI alone.

items.text: Restricted-Entry Interval
Restricted-Entry Interval — REI
This is the period that must pass before workers may enter a treated field without the specified personal protective equipment.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This is the period that must pass before workers may enter a treated field without the specified personal protective equipment.

items.text: It differs from the preharvest interval.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It differs from the preharvest interval.

items.text: The following should be recorded:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following should be recorded:

items: Date and time when entry is permitted.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Date and time when entry is permitted.

items.text: The REI is particularly important during:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The REI is particularly important during:

items.text: Spray Management Near Harvest
As harvest approaches, priority shifts toward:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]As harvest approaches, priority shifts toward:

items: Increasing reliance on suitable biological and mechanical methods.
Documenting the final treatment in each sector.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Increasing reliance on suitable biological and mechanical methods.
[[prepared]]Documenting the final treatment in each sector.

items.text: An active ingredient should not be used merely because another product has a long PHI. The selected material must be effective against the stage present and acceptable under regulatory requirements.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]An active ingredient should not be used merely because another product has a long PHI. The selected material must be effective against the stage present and acceptable under regulatory requirements.

items.text: Occupational Safety
Rational use includes protection of workers from exposure.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Rational use includes protection of workers from exposure.

items.text: Measures include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Measures include:

items: Avoiding eating or smoking during preparation.
Never mixing the spray solution by hand.
Disposing of containers through the approved system.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Never mixing the spray solution by hand.
[[prepared]]Disposing of containers through the approved system.

items.text: Pesticide containers should never be reused for storing water, food, or animal feed.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pesticide containers should never be reused for storing water, food, or animal feed.

items.text: Drift and Environmental Contamination
Drift occurs when spray droplets move beyond the target area.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Drift occurs when spray droplets move beyond the target area.

items.text: It increases with:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It increases with:

items.text: Drift may cause:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Drift may cause:

items: Loss of part of the applied dose.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Loss of part of the applied dose.

items.text: Buffer zones and label restrictions concerning water bodies, residences, and neighboring crops should be followed.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Buffer zones and label restrictions concerning water bodies, residences, and neighboring crops should be followed.

items.text: Management of Containers and Remaining Spray Mixture
The spray volume should be calculated to match the treated area and avoid excess mixture.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The spray volume should be calculated to match the treated area and avoid excess mixture.

items.text: Remaining spray should not be discharged into:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Remaining spray should not be discharged into:

items.text: Containers should be rinsed according to approved guidance and punctured or collected through the authorized disposal system to prevent reuse.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Containers should be rinsed according to approved guidance and punctured or collected through the authorized disposal system to prevent reuse.

items.text: Illustrative Rotation-Window Model
The season may be divided into thermal or time-based windows.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The season may be divided into thermal or time-based windows.

items.text: A theoretical example:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A theoretical example:

rows: Do not use A in the following window
prepared_rows: [[prepared]]Do not use A in the following window

items.text: The letters do not represent specific active ingredients. The actual program depends on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The letters do not represent specific active ingredients. The actual program depends on:

items.text: Chemical Program According to Infestation Stage
No Active Infestation
Limited Egg Presence

items: Prepare an intervention targeting hatching if required.
Do not use a broad-spectrum product without justification.

[[prepared]]Do not use a broad-spectrum product without justification.

items.text: Presence of L1 and L2

items: Select a registered active ingredient effective against young instars.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Select a registered active ingredient effective against young instars.

items: Review whether larvae have entered fruits.
Do not expect complete efficacy from an active ingredient unable to reach them.
Integrate treatment with collection of infested fruits and foci.

items: Intervene rapidly according to the threshold and registration.

items: Do not exceed the maximum number of applications.
Select tools compatible with delivery requirements.

items.text: Pesticide Record Template
The record should also include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The record should also include:

items: Number of previous applications of the group.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Number of previous applications of the group.

items.text: Indicators of Successful Chemical Control
These include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These include:

items: No need to repeat treatment because of poor execution.
Compliance with the allowed number of applications.
Reduced control cost per acceptable metric ton.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]No need to repeat treatment because of poor execution.
[[prepared]]Compliance with the allowed number of applications.
[[prepared]]Reduced control cost per acceptable metric ton.

items.text: A large number of dead larvae alone is insufficient if new infestation continues to appear.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A large number of dead larvae alone is insufficient if new infestation continues to appear.

items.text: Common Errors
Error One: Following a Calendar Spray Program
This causes unnecessary applications and high resistance pressure.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This causes unnecessary applications and high resistance pressure.

items.text: Error Two: Changing the Trade Name While Keeping the Same IRAC Group
This does not constitute rotation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This does not constitute rotation.

items.text: Error Three: Using One Group Against Successive Generations
This accelerates resistance selection.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This accelerates resistance selection.

items.text: Error Four: Spraying Larvae After They Have Entered Fruits
Protection is limited and damage has already occurred.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Protection is limited and damage has already occurred.

items.text: Error Five: Using a Dose Below the Label Rate
This may fail to kill moderately susceptible individuals and increase selection pressure.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This may fail to kill moderately susceptible individuals and increase selection pressure.

items.text: Error Six: Exceeding the Dose
This does not overcome resistance and increases residues and risk.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This does not overcome resistance and increases residues and risk.

items.text: Error Seven: Mixing Several Active Ingredients Without a Clear Purpose
This increases cost and problems without guaranteeing efficacy.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This increases cost and problems without guaranteeing efficacy.

items.text: Error Eight: Treating Every Mixture as a Resistance-Management Program
Both active ingredients should be effective and belong to different groups.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Both active ingredients should be effective and belong to different groups.

items.text: Error Nine: Repeating the Same Mixture
This exposes the pest repeatedly to the same two groups.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This exposes the pest repeatedly to the same two groups.

items.text: Error Ten: Neglecting Sprayer Calibration
Application failure may appear to be resistance.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Application failure may appear to be resistance.

items.text: Error Eleven: Using High Pressure Without Evaluating Deposition
This may increase drift instead of coverage.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This may increase drift instead of coverage.

items.text: Error Twelve: Neglecting Lower Leaf Surfaces and Growing Points
This leaves oviposition and hatching sites unprotected.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This leaves oviposition and hatching sites unprotected.

items.text: Error Thirteen: Judging Treatment by Old Mines
New mines and live larvae should be counted.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]New mines and live larvae should be counted.

items.text: Error Fourteen: Repeating the Spray Immediately When Rapid Mortality Is Not Observed
Speed of action differs among groups.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Speed of action differs among groups.

items.text: Error Fifteen: Ignoring Natural Enemies
Their mortality may cause pest resurgence.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Their mortality may cause pest resurgence.

items.text: Error Sixteen: Ignoring the PHI
This exposes the crop to rejection and residue problems.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This exposes the crop to rejection and residue problems.

items.text: Error Seventeen: Treating the MRL as an Optional Guideline
It is a legal or commercial limit that must be respected.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It is a legal or commercial limit that must be respected.

items.text: Error Eighteen: Declaring Resistance Without Testing
Application errors should be excluded and susceptibility evaluated.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Application errors should be excluded and susceptibility evaluated.

items.text: Practical Priorities for Open-Field Tomatoes in Egypt
Priorities are:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Priorities are:

items: 1. Do not implement any treatment without monitoring.
2. Review current Egyptian registration before and during the season.
3. Record the IRAC group number for every active ingredient.
4. Do not repeat the same group against successive generations.
6. Recalibrate the sprayer as plant size changes.
7. Improve coverage of growing points and lower leaf surfaces.
8. Do not treat different trade names as rotation.
10. Link every treatment to harvest timing.
11. Align the program with the supply or export market.
12. Do not base the program on organophosphates and pyrethroids.
13. Avoid excessive use of diamides, spinosyns, or avermectins.
15. Collect samples for susceptibility testing when failure is repeated.
16. Manage old fields and immigration sources.
17. Do not use insecticides to compensate for poor sanitation or absence of traps.
18. Maintain a complete treatment record beginning in the nursery.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]1. Do not implement any treatment without monitoring.
[[prepared]]2. Review current Egyptian registration before and during the season.
[[prepared]]3. Record the IRAC group number for every active ingredient.
[[prepared]]4. Do not repeat the same group against successive generations.
[[prepared]]6. Recalibrate the sprayer as plant size changes.
[[prepared]]7. Improve coverage of growing points and lower leaf surfaces.
[[prepared]]8. Do not treat different trade names as rotation.
[[prepared]]10. Link every treatment to harvest timing.
[[prepared]]11. Align the program with the supply or export market.
[[prepared]]12. Do not base the program on organophosphates and pyrethroids.
[[prepared]]13. Avoid excessive use of diamides, spinosyns, or avermectins.
[[prepared]]15. Collect samples for susceptibility testing when failure is repeated.
[[prepared]]16. Manage old fields and immigration sources.
[[prepared]]17. Do not use insecticides to compensate for poor sanitation or absence of traps.
[[prepared]]18. Maintain a complete treatment record beginning in the nursery.

items.text: Summary of Section Ten
Chemical control against Tuta absoluta should be used as a targeted intervention within integrated pest management rather than as a fixed periodic program.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Chemical control against Tuta absoluta should be used as a targeted intervention within [[term:ipm]]integrated pest management[[/term]] rather than as a fixed periodic program.

items.text: The decision depends on monitoring, dominant pest stage, crop stage, natural-enemy activity, and harvest timing.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The decision depends on monitoring, dominant pest stage, crop stage, natural-enemy activity, and harvest timing.

items.text: Highest efficacy is achieved when eggs close to hatching and first- and second-instar larvae are targeted before they enter growing points, stems, and fruits.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Highest efficacy is achieved when eggs close to hatching and first- and second-instar larvae are targeted before they enter growing points, stems, and fruits.

items.text: Resistance-management programs should be based on IRAC group numbers rather than trade names.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Resistance-management programs should be based on IRAC group numbers rather than trade names.

items.text: Important examples include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Important examples include:

items: Chlorantraniliprole, Cyantraniliprole, Flubendiamide, and Tetraniliprole: group 28.

items.text: Rotation between two active ingredients within the same group does not constitute true rotation.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Rotation between two active ingredients within the same group does not constitute true rotation.

items.text: Resistance management recommends avoiding exposure of successive generations to the same group and using a window strategy aligned with generation duration and local temperature.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Resistance management recommends avoiding exposure of successive generations to the same group and using a window strategy aligned with generation duration and local temperature.

items.text: Mixtures are not substitutes for rotation. Each active ingredient in the mixture should be effective, registered, and belong to a different group, and the same mixture should not be repeated throughout the season.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Mixtures are not substitutes for rotation. Each active ingredient in the mixture should be effective, registered, and belong to a different group, and the same mixture should not be repeated throughout the season.

items.text: Resistance must also be distinguished from application failure. Failure may result from timing, coverage, dose, weather, immigration, or larvae already being inside fruits.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Resistance must also be distinguished from application failure. Failure may result from timing, coverage, dose, weather, immigration, or larvae already being inside fruits.

items.text: Susceptibility can be scientifically verified using standardized tests, including IRAC Method No. 022 for Tuta absoluta larvae.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Susceptibility can be scientifically verified using standardized tests, including IRAC Method No. 022 for Tuta absoluta larvae.

items.text: Rational chemical control is completed by compliance with preharvest intervals, maximum residue limits, restricted-entry intervals, worker safety, and protection of the environment and natural enemies.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Rational chemical control is completed by compliance with preharvest intervals, maximum residue limits, restricted-entry intervals, worker safety, and protection of the environment and natural enemies.
